title: Fagaropsis angolensis
intro: Fagaropsis angolensis, also known as the Marula tree, is a deciduous tree species native to southern Africa, belonging to the family Burseraceae. The Marula tree has been traditionally used in African folk medicine for its various health benefits, including treating fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal issues, as well as serving as a natural anti-inflammatory agent. The bioactive constituents of Fagaropsis angolensis include triterpenoids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with the Marula tree include teas, infusions, and extracts, which are often used to treat a range of health conditions, including digestive issues and skin problems.
taxonomy: Fagaropsis angolensis is classified as a species of tree in the family Burseraceae. It belongs to the genus Fagaropsis, which is part of the order Sapindales. This classification indicates its evolutionary relationship with other plants in the same family and order. The family Burseraceae is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the candlewood family. Fagaropsis angolensis is a unique species within this classification, native to southern Africa.
